questions.question: A game collection contains only cards and tiles. Each card shows 1 symbol, and each tile shows 4 symbols. Exactly 40% of the collection are tiles. If there are 264 symbols altogether, how many items are in the collection?
questions.solution:
Let x = total items. Tiles = 0.4x, cards = 0.6x.
Symbols: 0.6x √ó 1 + 0.4x √ó 4 = 0.6x + 1.6x = 2.2x = 264
x = 264 / 2.2 = 120 items.

questions.question:
X^(a/b) means: take the b-th root of X, then raise to the a-th power. Equivalently, (ŠĶá‚ąöX)ŠĶÉ.

Example 1: What is 81^(5/4)?
Example 2: What is 32^(3/5)?
questions.notes: Fractional exponents ‚ÄĒ numerator is power, denominator is root. Related to discomfort with ‚ąö3¬≥ on AMC 8 2025 Q8.
questions.solution:
Example 1: 81^(5/4) ‚ÄĒ 4th root of 81 = 3, then 3‚ĀĶ = 243.
Example 2: 32^(3/5) ‚ÄĒ 5th root of 32 = 2, then 2¬≥ = 8.

questions.question:
C(n, k) = n! / (k! ¬∑ (n‚ąík)!) counts the number of ways to choose k items from n, where order doesn't matter.

Example 1: How many ways can you choose 3 students from a group of 8?
Example 2: A pizza menu offers 10 toppings. How many different 4-topping pizzas can you make?
questions.notes: Combination formula ‚ÄĒ distinguish from permutations (where order matters). Related to Pascal's triangle.

questions.question:
The Shoelace Theorem finds the area of a polygon from its vertex coordinates. List vertices in order, then compute:
Area = ¬Ĺ |x‚āĀ(y‚āā‚ąíy‚āô) + x‚āā(y‚āÉ‚ąíy‚āĀ) + x‚āÉ(y‚āĄ‚ąíy‚āā) + ... + x‚āô(y‚āĀ‚ąíy‚āô‚āč‚āĀ)|

Example 1: Find the area of the triangle with vertices (1, 1), (4, 1), (4, 5).
Example 2: Find the area of the quadrilateral with vertices (0, 0), (4, 0), (3, 3), (1, 4).
questions.notes: Shoelace formula ‚ÄĒ useful when vertices are given as coordinates. Cross-multiply diagonals down-right and down-left, subtract, halve. Also called the surveyor's formula.

questions.question:
Five integers are written around a circle so that no two or three adjacent numbers have a sum divisible by 3. How many of these five numbers are divisible by 3?
A) 0   B) 1   C) 2   D) 3   E) Impossible to determine

Example 2: Seven integers are written around a circle so that no two or three adjacent numbers have a sum divisible by 3. How many must be divisible by 3?
Example 3: Four integers are written around a circle so that no two adjacent numbers have a sum divisible by 3. Can all four be divisible by 3?
questions.notes: Modular arithmetic on circular arrangements. Think in residues mod 3 ‚ÄĒ constraint forces a pattern on the residues.
questions.solution:
Think in residues mod 3: {0, 1, 2}. "No two adjacent sum to 0 mod 3" bans pairs (0,0), (1,2), (2,1). So adjacent numbers must share the same nonzero residue, or one is 0 and the other nonzero.

Original (5 on circle): The 0-residues can't be adjacent. Working through: exactly 2 must be divisible by 3. Answer: C.

Example 2 (7 on circle): Same logic ‚ÄĒ 2 must be divisible by 3.

Example 3 (4 on circle): If all four ‚Č° 0 mod 3, every adjacent pair sums to 0 mod 3. So no ‚ÄĒ at most 2 of 4 can be divisible by 3.

questions.question:
The sum of interior angles of an n-sided polygon is (n ‚ąí 2) √ó 180¬į.

Example 1: What is the sum of interior angles of a hexagon (6 sides)?
Example 2: A polygon has interior angles that sum to 1440¬į. How many sides does it have?
Example 3: What is each interior angle of a regular decagon (10 sides)?
questions.notes: Fundamental polygon angle formula. Derived by splitting any n-gon into (n ‚ąí 2) triangles from one vertex. For regular polygons, each angle = (n ‚ąí 2) √ó 180¬į / n.
questions.solution:
Example 1: n = 6. Sum = (6 ‚ąí 2) √ó 180¬į = 4 √ó 180¬į = 720¬į.
Example 2: (n ‚ąí 2) √ó 180 = 1440 ‚Üí n ‚ąí 2 = 8 ‚Üí n = 10. It's a decagon.
Example 3: n = 10. Sum = (10 ‚ąí 2) √ó 180¬į = 1440¬į. Each angle = 1440¬į / 10 = 144¬į.

questions.title: Simplifying ‚ąö(c + d‚ąön)
questions.question:
Step 1: Expand (p + q‚ąö2)¬≤. What form does the result take?

Step 2: Now simplify ‚ąö(3 + 2‚ąö2). (Hint: your answer to Step 1 tells you what form to guess.)

Example 2: Simplify ‚ąö(5 ‚ąí 2‚ąö6).
questions.notes: Christopher needs to discover the a + b‚ąön form himself by squaring first, rather than being told the answer format. Step 1 builds the mental model, Step 2 applies it.
questions.solution:
Step 1: (p + q‚ąö2)¬≤ = p¬≤ + 2pq‚ąö2 + 2q¬≤. This is (p¬≤ + 2q¬≤) + 2pq‚ąö2 ‚ÄĒ a rational part plus an irrational part. So the square root of something in that form should give back p + q‚ąö2.

Step 2: ‚ąö(3 + 2‚ąö2). From Step 1, guess (p + q‚ąö2)¬≤ = 3 + 2‚ąö2. Match: p¬≤ + 2q¬≤ = 3 and 2pq = 2, so pq = 1. Try p = 1, q = 1: 1 + 2 = 3 ‚úď. Answer: 1 + ‚ąö2.

Example 2: ‚ąö(5 ‚ąí 2‚ąö6). Guess (‚ąöa ‚ąí ‚ąöb)¬≤. Try (‚ąö3 ‚ąí ‚ąö2)¬≤ = 3 + 2 ‚ąí 2‚ąö6 = 5 ‚ąí 2‚ąö6 ‚úď. Answer: ‚ąö3 ‚ąí ‚ąö2.

questions.title: Breaking up nth roots: ‚ĀŅ‚ąö(ab)
questions.question:
Simplify each expression by breaking it into separate roots.

Example 1: ¬≥‚ąö(8 √ó 27)
Example 2: ‚ąö(50)
Example 3: ‚Āī‚ąö(16 √ó 81)
questions.notes: Tests whether Christopher remembers that ‚ĀŅ‚ąö(a¬∑b) = ‚ĀŅ‚ąöa √ó ‚ĀŅ‚ąöb. The question deliberately does NOT state the rule ‚ÄĒ he should recall and apply it himself.

questions.title: Negative exponents: 1/a‚ĀŅ = a‚ĀĽ‚ĀŅ
questions.question:
Rewrite each expression as a single power (with a possibly negative exponent).

Example 1: 1/5¬≥ = 5^?
Example 2: 1/2‚Āī = 2^?
Example 3: (1/4)‚Āī ‚ÄĒ rewrite everything as a power of 2.
Example 4: (1/3)‚ĀĶ ‚ÄĒ rewrite as a power of 3.
questions.notes: Christopher had major trouble seeing that 1/4 = 2‚ĀĽ¬≤ (and therefore working with negative exponents). This drills the key identity 1/a‚ĀŅ = a‚ĀĽ‚ĀŅ, then applies it in multi-step problems like (1/4)‚Āī = 2^x.
questions.solution:
Key rule: 1/a‚ĀŅ = a‚ĀĽ‚ĀŅ.

Example 1: 1/5¬≥ = 5‚ĀĽ¬≥.
Example 2: 1/2‚Āī = 2‚ĀĽ‚Āī.
Example 3: (1/4)‚Āī. First, 1/4 = 4‚ĀĽ¬Ļ = (2¬≤)‚ĀĽ¬Ļ = 2‚ĀĽ¬≤. So (1/4)‚Āī = (2‚ĀĽ¬≤)‚Āī = 2‚ĀĽ‚Āł.
Example 4: (1/3)‚ĀĶ = (3‚ĀĽ¬Ļ)‚ĀĶ = 3‚ĀĽ‚ĀĶ.

questions.title: Arithmetic mean vs. geometric mean
questions.question:
Part A: Define the geometric mean.
‚ÄĒ For two numbers a and b, the geometric mean is ___.
‚ÄĒ For three numbers a, b, and c, the geometric mean is ___.

Part B: For positive numbers, which is always greater ‚ÄĒ the arithmetic mean or the geometric mean? Is there any exception?

Example: Compute both means for {2, 8}. Then for {5, 5}.
questions.notes: AM-GM inequality is a classic competition tool. Key caveat: AM ‚Č• GM always, with equality only when all numbers are the same. Christopher should know both the definitions and the inequality.
questions.solution:
Part A:
‚ÄĒ For a, b: geometric mean = ‚ąö(a¬∑b).
‚ÄĒ For a, b, c: geometric mean = ¬≥‚ąö(a¬∑b¬∑c).
‚ÄĒ In general for a‚āĀ‚Ä¶a‚āô: geometric mean = ‚ĀŅ‚ąö(a‚āĀ¬∑a‚āā¬∑‚Ä¶¬∑a‚āô).

Part B: The arithmetic mean is always ‚Č• the geometric mean (AM-GM inequality). They are equal ONLY when all the numbers are the same.

Example {2, 8}: AM = (2+8)/2 = 5. GM = ‚ąö(2¬∑8) = ‚ąö16 = 4. AM > GM ‚úď.
Example {5, 5}: AM = 5. GM = ‚ąö(5¬∑5) = 5. AM = GM (equal because the numbers are the same).

questions.title: Nested radical expressions
questions.question:
Simplify each expression to a single number.

Example 1: [‚ąö((‚ąö5)¬≥)]‚Āī
Example 2: [‚ąö((‚ąö3)‚ĀĶ)]¬≤
Example 3: [¬≥‚ąö((‚ąö2)‚ĀĻ)]¬≤
questions.notes: Based on AMC 8 2025 Q5 style ‚ÄĒ nested radicals with an outer exponent. Convert everything to fractional exponents and multiply. Christopher should practice chaining exponent rules on radicals.

questions.title: Distributing exponents: (ab)‚ĀŅ = a‚ĀŅ √ó b‚ĀŅ
questions.question:
Expand and simplify. Remember: when raising a product to a power, the exponent applies to EVERY factor.

Example 1: (5 √ó ¬≥‚ąö2)¬≥
Example 2: (4 √ó ‚ąö3)¬≤
Example 3: (2 √ó ‚ĀĶ‚ąö7)‚ĀĶ
questions.notes: Christopher made the error of only cubing the radical part and forgetting to cube the coefficient (e.g., computing 6 √ó (¬≥‚ąö3)¬≥ = 6 √ó 3 = 18 instead of 6¬≥ √ó 3 = 648). This drills the rule (a¬∑b)‚ĀŅ = a‚ĀŅ ¬∑ b‚ĀŅ.
questions.solution:
Key rule: (a ¬∑ b)‚ĀŅ = a‚ĀŅ ¬∑ b‚ĀŅ ‚ÄĒ the exponent hits EVERY factor.

Example 1: (5 √ó ¬≥‚ąö2)¬≥ = 5¬≥ √ó (¬≥‚ąö2)¬≥ = 125 √ó 2 = 250.

Example 2: (4 √ó ‚ąö3)¬≤ = 4¬≤ √ó (‚ąö3)¬≤ = 16 √ó 3 = 48.

Example 3: (2 √ó ‚ĀĶ‚ąö7)‚ĀĶ = 2‚ĀĶ √ó (‚ĀĶ‚ąö7)‚ĀĶ = 32 √ó 7 = 224.

General form: (a ¬∑ b)‚ĀŅ = a‚ĀŅ ¬∑ b‚ĀŅ. Never raise only one factor!

questions.question:
For x¬≤ + ax + b = 0, express a and b in terms of the roots.

Example 1: roots are 3 and 5 ‚Üí find a and b
Example 2: roots are ‚ąí2 and 7 ‚Üí find a and b
Example 3: roots are ‚ąí4 and ‚ąí1 ‚Üí find a and b

(Rederive if needed: factor as (x ‚ąí r‚āĀ)(x ‚ąí r‚āā) and expand.)
questions.notes: Vieta's formulas for monic quadratics. Common mistake: forgetting the sign flip on a (it's negative the sum, not the sum).
questions.solution:
a = ‚ąí(sum of roots),  b = product of roots

Example 1: a = ‚ąí(3+5) = ‚ąí8,  b = 3√ó5 = 15
Example 2: a = ‚ąí(‚ąí2+7) = ‚ąí5,  b = ‚ąí2√ó7 = ‚ąí14
Example 3: a = ‚ąí(‚ąí4‚ąí1) = 5,  b = ‚ąí4√ó(‚ąí1) = 4
